title: Subgroup Tests and the Aldous--Lyons Conjecture
speaker: Michael Chapman
abstract:
A common theme in mathematics is that limits of finite objects are well behaved. This allows one to prove many theorems about finitely approximable objects, while leaving the general case open --- examples for this are Gottschalk's conjecture, Kaplansky's direct finiteness conjecture, and many more. When the topology of the space is somewhat coarse, it becomes very hard to decide whether every object is approximable by finite ones, or whether there exist non-approximable objects. Some of the more famous problems in various fields of mathematics can be framed this way; this includes Connes' embedding problem (CEP) in functional analysis, the Aldous--Lyons conjecture in probability theory, the soficity problem of Gromov--Weiss in group theory, and more. 
In 2019, Ji--Natarajan--Vidick--Wright--Yuen resolved CEP in the negative, namely, they showed that there are non-approximable characters of the free group. The amazing thing about their result is that it is deduced from complexity theory, and specifically from undecidability in certain (quantum) interactive proof systems. Inspired by their work, we suggest a novel interactive proof system which is related to the Aldous--Lyons conjecture in the following way: If the Aldous--Lyons conjecture was true, then every language in this interactive proof system is decidable. A key concept we introduce for this purpose is that of a Subgroup Test, which is our analogue of a Non-local Game. By providing a reduction from the Halting Problem to this new proof system, we refute the Aldous-Lyons conjecture.
In this first talk we introduce the main concepts, motivate the conjecture and give a high level description of the proof strategy. No special mathematical background will be assumed.
This talk is based on a joint work with Lewis Bowen, Alex Lubotzky and Thomas Vidick.

proof_stages.label: Refutation claim and route
proof_stages.summary: The talk states the joint refutation of the Aldous-Lyons conjecture and lays out a route through graph limits, invariant random subgroups, characters, and an interactive-proof/complexity-theoretic decision problem.

proof_stages.label: Cellular-automaton motivation for soficity
proof_stages.summary: Local recoloring rules and Gottschalk's injective-implies-surjective question motivate why Benjamini-Schramm limits of finite graphs, or sofic networks/groups, are a natural class: finite graphs satisfy the property trivially and Gromov-Weiss extend it to these limits.

proof_stages.label: Benjamini-Schramm convergence formalism
proof_stages.summary: Rooted bounded-degree graphs are topologized by agreement of finite balls, random rooted graphs are probability measures on this compact space, and convergence is defined by convergence of all finite-radius ball distributions, with finite graphs viewed via a uniform random root.

proof_stages.label: Unimodular networks and the Aldous-Lyons target
proof_stages.summary: After recalling results such as recurrence of planar limits and sofic networks being unimodular, the Aldous-Lyons question is formulated as whether every unimodular network is sofic, and the desired refutation is framed as a strict separation of the sofic and unimodular convex sets.

proof_stages.label: Invariant random subgroups and finite describability
proof_stages.summary: The subgroup space of a group is equipped with the conjugation action, IRSs are its invariant probability measures, finite actions produce finitely describable IRSs via stabilizers, and the corresponding closure question is posed, especially for the free group.

proof_stages.label: Schreier-graph bridge to the free-group case
proof_stages.summary: Group actions and subgroup coset actions are converted into rooted Schreier graphs, and Bowen's theorem is used to identify IRSs with unimodular networks and finite-describable IRS limits with sofic networks, making the free-group IRS approximation problem a special case of Aldous-Lyons.

proof_stages.label: Character/Connes-embedding analogy and subgroup-test strategy
proof_stages.summary: Characters and finite-dimensional characters provide a parallel closure problem whose negative solution follows from the MIP* undecidability route to Connes embedding, motivating the promised subgroup-test complexity class in which Aldous-Lyons would imply decidability but the class is shown undecidable.

examples.name: Conway's Game of Life
examples.note: The Game of Life is used as a concrete local recoloring system on the two-dimensional grid, with its video, gliders, and all-alive/all-dead configurations illustrating locality, complex behavior, and non-injectivity rather than generating the proof idea.
examples.engagement_reason: He specifies the grid, neighborhood, colors, update rules, shows the evolution, and checks non-injectivity using all-live versus all-dead initial states.

passes.reason: A standard noninjective cellular automaton used illustratively, not a classification exception or a refuting instance.

examples.note: The regular tree example contrasts finite balls, which fail because of boundary leaves, with high-girth regular graphs, whose fixed-radius neighborhoods become tree-like.
examples.engagement_reason: He explains why balls in the regular tree fail as finite approximants and then checks that large-girth regular graphs have the correct radius-r balls eventually.

examples.name: Two- versus three-dimensional lattice recurrence
examples.note: The speaker recalls that simple random walk on Z^2 is recurrent while on Z^3 it is not, using the drunk-man/drunk-bird joke to clarify the recurrence conclusion in Benjamini-Schramm's planar-limit theorem.
examples.engagement_reason: He cites the known recurrence of the two-dimensional grid and transience of the three-dimensional grid only as illustrative facts.

passes.reason: The symmetric lattices sit at the critical recurrence/transience dimension threshold, with the three-dimensional case showing recurrence fails.

examples.name: Normal subgroup as a Dirac invariant random subgroup
examples.note: A normal subgroup gives a conjugation-invariant point mass on the subgroup space, and it later reappears in the question of whether a normal-subgroup IRS outside the finite-describable closure would produce a non-sofic quotient.
examples.engagement_reason: He verifies that the Dirac measure on a normal subgroup is invariant because conjugation leaves the subgroup fixed.

passes.reason: A Dirac IRS is a collapsed point-mass distribution at a conjugation-fixed normal subgroup.
